following_context: In this chapter, we develop a systematic framework to derive a new class of three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ dualities, which we refer to as \emph{chiral-planar mirror dualities} [Footnote: This was historically called the \textit{Dorito Duality} by the author. Never forget what was taken from us.] . These dualities relate non-abelian chiral gauge theories with Chern-Simons interactions to purely abelian, planar quiver gauge theories, and arise as infrared limits of real mass deformations of known $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pairs .

section_title: Mirror Duals of \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=2$}{3d N=2} Unitary Chern-Simons-Matter Theories
content: In this chapter, we develop a systematic framework to derive a new class of three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ dualities, which we refer to as \emph{chiral-planar mirror dualities} [Footnote: This was historically called the \textit{Dorito Duality} by the author. Never forget what was taken from us.] . These dualities relate non-abelian chiral gauge theories with Chern-Simons interactions to purely abelian, planar quiver gauge theories, and arise as infrared limits of real mass deformations of known $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pairs . Our starting point is the observation that many $\mathcal{N}=2$ chiral theories of interest can be obtained from $\mathcal{N}=4$ gauge theories by turning on a real mass deformation for the axial symmetry \[ U(1)_\tau = U(1)_{H-C}, \] which breaks supersymmetry from $\mathcal{N}=4$ to $\mathcal{N}=2$ . A prototypical example is provided by three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $F$ hypermultiplets. By combining the axial real mass deformation with appropriate shifts along the Coulomb branch, this theory flows to a chiral $\mathcal{N}=2$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $F$ fundamental chiral multiplets and nontrivial Chern Simons levels. Since the original $\mathcal{N}=4$ theory admits a mirror description in terms of a linear quiver , one may attempt to perform the same deformation on the dual side. However, the mirror theory contains many gauge nodes and hence many Coulomb branch directions, leading to a large set of candidate vacua. Identifying the vacuum that correctly reproduces the dual of the chiral SQCD is therefore highly nontrivial. A powerful tool to address this problem is provided by the exact partition function on the squashed three-sphere $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ . As shown in , real mass deformations correspond to controlled large-mass limits of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function. In such limits, the partition function factorizes into a finite contribution—associated with the interacting infrared theory—and a highly oscillatory phase. Crucially, this phase depends on how the real mass limit is combined with shifts of the gauge fugacities, and therefore encodes the choice of vacuum on the Coulomb branch. As a consequence, matching the oscillatory phases on the two sides of a known $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror identity provides a sharp criterion to identify the pair of infrared theories that remain dual after the deformation. In this chapter, we apply this strategy in detail to the case of $U(N)$ SQCD. Starting from the equality of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition functions of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pair, we perform a systematic real mass deformation and show how to isolate the vacuum that reproduces the chiral $\mathcal{N}=2$ SQCD on the electric side. Remarkably, we find that the corresponding magnetic theory is a \emph{planar} [Footnote: The dual theory is planar, in the sense that it is a quiver drawn on a plane, with a cubic superpotential term for each closed oriented loop, as in the 4d periodic quivers associated to the dimers of \cite{Hanany:2005ve, Franco:2005sm}, or in the minimally supersymmetric AdS$_5$/CFT$_4$ correspondence studied in \cite{Benvenuti:2005cz, Benvenuti:2005ja, Franco:2005rj, Butti:2005sw}.] \emph{abelian quiver} , obtained by fully Higgsing each $U(k)$ gauge node of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror theory down to its Cartan subgroup. The resulting dual theory exhibits nontrivial mixed Chern Simons couplings and a rich pattern of topological symmetry enhancement, inherited from the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror symmetry. Beyond establishing this specific duality, the analysis presented here highlights a general structural feature: real mass deformations of $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pairs naturally lead to chiral planar mirror dualities, in which mesonic symmetries are mapped to topological symmetries and abelian gauge dynamics emerge in a planar arrangement. While the deformation procedure itself is technically involved, it provides valuable guidance for identifying the correct infrared dual and motivates the search for a more systematic construction. This motivation leads naturally to the algorithmic approach developed in the subsequent half of the chapter, where we extend the mirror dualization algorithm to the chiral planar setting. The detailed partition-function analysis presented here thus serves a dual purpose: it establishes the validity of the chiral planar mirror duality in a concrete example, and it provides the conceptual and technical foundation for the algorithmic framework that follows.

section_title: General Yoga of Planar Abelianization
content: We pick up where we left off in the previous chapter while reviewing 3d $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror symmetry in $U(N)$ gauge theories with $F$ hypermultiplets, shown in the top row of Figure . We remind the reader we are working in the $\mathcal{N}=2^*$ set-up to effectively study supersymmetry breaking real mass deformations. Thus, we set $U(1)_R = U(1)_{C+H}$ as the R-symmetry group, where $U(1)_C\in SU(2)_C$ and $U(1)_H\in SU(2)_H$ of the non-abelian $\mathcal{N}=4$ R-symmetry $SU(2)_H\times SU(2)_C$ . The commutant of $U(1)_R$ , which is $U(1)_\tau=U(1)_{C-H}$ , is a global symmetry from the point of view of the $\mathcal{N}=2$ theory. Thus, the global symmetries of both theories is $SU(F)\times U(1)_{top}\times U(1)_{\tau}$ . We now consider a supersymmetry breaking mass deformation. This choice is not arbitrary, and we do so by turning on a non-zero vacuum expectation value (VEV) for the real scalar component of the background vector multiplet associated with the $U(1)_\tau$ symmetry. The VEV induces a mass term for every chiral superfield proportional to its charge under the symmetry. Recalling that $U(1)_\tau$ is a flavor subgroup of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ R-symmetry; hence, the deformation performed has the effect of breaking the R-symmetry from $SU(2)_C \times SU(2)_H \to U(1)_R$ , and supersymmetry is broken from $\mathcal{N}=4$ to $\mathcal{N}=2$ . In the most generic vacuum, the resulting IR theory would be a topological quantum field theory. However, we follow the flow to distinguished points on the Coulomb branch where the theory is an interacting one instead. While non-trivial, this can be done effectively at the level of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function and the equations of motion . However, before doing this, we will attempt to qualitatively motivate this picture first. We first consider the \textit{electric} " $\mathcal{N}=4$ SQCD theory. We move on the Coulomb branch in such a way to reach an interacting vacuum where all the fundamental chiral fields $Q$ remain massless while the adjoint $\Phi$ and the $F$ anti-fundamental chiral fields $\widetilde{Q}$ acquire a mass, negative and positive respectively, and are integrated out [Footnote: One can also consider different interacting vacua where a certain number of fundamental and anti-fundamental fields remain massless. While this problem is tractable, we will tackle the case of SQCD theories with more general matter content in a different, algorithmic way in Section \ref{sec: Examples}.] . The procedure of integrating out chiral fields produces CS interactions due to fermionic modes becoming massive. The resulting theory is the $\mathcal{N}=2$ $U(N)_{-\frac{F}{2}+N,-\frac{F}{2}}$ SQCD depicted on the bottom-left panel of Figure . Here, $U(N)_{k,k+\ell N}$ [Footnote: We follow the standard notation for Chern-Simons levels: $U(N)_{(k_1,k_2)} = \frac{SU(N)_{k_1} \times U(1)_{N k_2} }{\mathbb{Z}_{N}}$. To avoid anomalies $k_1 \in \mathbb{Z}$ and the $U(1)$ level must be of the form $k_2 = k_1 + \ell N$, with $\ell \in \mathbb{Z}$.] denotes a $U(N)$ gauge group with CS terms:

following_context: where $A$ is the $U(N)$ gauge field. Each massive anti-fundamental multiplet, with negative mass, contributes as $(-\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2})$ to the CS level, and the massive adjoint chiral, with positive mass, contributes $(N,0)$ . Notice in particular that we have $\ell=-1$ .

section_title: General Yoga of Planar Abelianization
content: Schematic depiction of the $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror duality (bottom row) observed between $U(N)$ SQCD with $[F,0]$ fundamental fields and a planar, Abelian quiver gauge theory obtained by adding a suitably chosen real mass deformation to $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $[F,F]$ flavors and its mirror dual (top row). The electric $\mathcal{N}=2$ non-Abelian SQCD has CS level $(-\frac{F}{2}+N, -\frac{F}{2})$ and a vanishing superpotential, whereas the mirror $\mathcal{N}=2$ Abelian, planar quiver has CS and mixed CS interactions, a planar superpotential inherited from the $\mathcal{N}=4$ parent theory, and a monopole superpotential generated via the Polyakov Mechanism \cite{Polyakov1977} (captured by $\mathcal{W}_{mirror}$). Flavor and topological symmetries are exchanged by the duality, such that disorder operators (monopoles) are mapped to more familiar mesonic operators.

The electric $\mathcal{N}=2$ non-Abelian SQCD has CS level $(-\frac{F}{2}+N, -\frac{F}{2})$ and a vanishing superpotential, whereas the mirror $\mathcal{N}=2$ Abelian, planar quiver has CS and mixed CS interactions, a planar superpotential inherited from the $\mathcal{N}=4$ parent theory, and a monopole superpotential generated via the Polyakov Mechanism \cite{Polyakov1977} (captured by $\mathcal{W}_{mirror}$). Flavor and topological symmetries are exchanged by the duality, such that disorder operators (monopoles) are mapped to more familiar mesonic operators.}

section_title: General Yoga of Planar Abelianization
content:
On the left hand side, we present the $\mathcal{N}=2$ $U(N)_{-\frac{F}{2}+N,\,-\frac{F}{2}}$ SQCD with $F$ fundamental chiral multiplets, obtained by a real mass deformation of the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ SQCD. On the right, we show its planar, mirror-like dual description.
Each gauge node carries Chern-Simons level $+1$ (indicated by red labels).
Mixed ChernSimons interactions (green labels) are present for every pair of gauge nodes connected by a link, with level $-1$ for diagonal links and $+1$ for vertical links.
FI parameters are indicated in orange. On the SQCD side, the fundamental chiral multiplets are assigned trial R-charge $1$.
On the planar side, diagonal chiral fields have trial R-charge $0$, while vertical chiral fields have trial R-charge $2$.
Diagonal chirals oriented from SE to NW connect $N$ gauge nodes, whereas diagonal chirals oriented from SW to NE connect $F-N$ gauge nodes.

content: where $A$ is the $U(N)$ gauge field. Each massive anti-fundamental multiplet, with negative mass, contributes as $(-\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2})$ to the CS level, and the massive adjoint chiral, with positive mass, contributes $(N,0)$ . Notice in particular that we have $\ell=-1$ . In the magnetic theory (top right panel of Figure ), under the same deformation, something interesting happens - we propose that the magnetic vacuum corresponding to the electric theory is such that each gauge node $U(k)$ is Higgsed to its maximal torus $U(1)^k$ producing a column of $k$ black $U(1)$ gauge nodes, while some chiral fields remain massless (as shown in the bottom right panel of Figure ). We present the duality (with refined fugacities) in Figure . A few comments regarding the planar dual in the righ panel of Figure are in order: \item There is a cubic superpotential term for every triangular loop. There is one term with $-1$ ( $+1$ ) coefficient for each clock-wise (anti-clock-wise) closed triangle. The cubic terms in the superpotential are a remnant of the cubic $\mathcal{N}=4$ superpotential that are preserved by the deformation. We denote these superpotential terms in short as $\mathcal{W}_{\text{planar}}$ . The diagonal/vertical chiral multiplets have trial R-charges $r,\,2-2r$ , respectively, compatible with the superpotential. \item $\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}$ are the monopole terms in the superpotential generated by the Polyakov mechanism due to the Higgsing of a $U(k)$ gauge symmetry to $U(1)^k$ . The exact collection of terms can be obtained with the following schematic rule. For each vertical arrow, there is a linear superpotential for the monopole with GNO flux $-1$ and $+1$ under the nodes connected by the arrow, from top to bottom. As a consequence of these monopole superpotential terms, the topological symmetry is broken to $U(1)^{F-1}$ which is expected to enhance in the infrared to $SU(F)$ , thus matching the global symmetry on the electric side. The FI terms of the $U(1)$ gauge nodes are compatible with the monopole superpotential terms. With the choice of trial R-symmetry described above, the $\alpha^{th}$ gauge node in the $I^{th}$ column of the planar quiver ( $\alpha=1,\dots, |G^{(I)}|$ , and $|G^{(I)}|$ is the rank of the corresponding gauge node in the $\mathcal{N}=4$ quiver, or equivalently, the number of $U(1)$ gauge nodes in the $I^{th}$ column of the $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver) has FI terms equal to $X_{I+1}-X_I+\frac{iQ}{4}(\delta_{\alpha,1}-\delta_{\alpha,|G^{(I)}|})$ . In Figure we only report the $X_I$ -dependent part of the FIs. \item CS interactions are present. Self and mixed-interactions can be encoded in a matrix $k_{ab}$ such that:

section_title: Global Symmetries and Gauge Invariant Operators
content: We now comment on the features of the duality described above. As a preliminary check, we compute the \textbf{Superconformal Index} . This guarantees that a number of properties are correctly reproduced by the dual theory such as - the presence of conserved currents associated to the correct global symmetry, and the properties of the operators in the chiral ring. We do this for $N=2, \, F=4,5,6$ and $N=3 \,, F=6$ , and report the results in Table . Notice that in order to perform the matching, it is important to have the mirror theory parameterized as in Figure . In particular the mixing between the R-symmetry and the topological symmetries is crucial for the enhancement of the global symmetry to be seen from the Superconformal Index. \centering

section_title: Global Symmetries and Gauge Invariant Operators
content: Matching Global Symmetries The global symmetry of the SQCD theory is, up to discrete factors, $SU(F)\times U(1)_\eta$ . On the mirror side, we can match the rank of the global symmetry as follows - in the absence of the monopole superpotential there is a $U(1)$ topological symmetry for each $U(1)$ gauge node. The monopole superpotential breaks the topological symmetries associated to the nodes in a column of the quiver to a diagonal $U(1)$ . Therefore there is a $U(1)^{F-1}$ unbroken topological symmetry associated to the $F-1$ columns of the planar quiver. Furthermore, there is a single $U(1)_\eta$ flavor symmetry which is preserved by the planar superpotential. In total, the rank of the global symmetry is $F$ , matching the SQCD side. The mirror-like duality implies that the $U(1)^F$ symmetry of the quiver theory is enhanced to $SU(F)\times U(1)$ , as can be seen from the index calculations as well.

following_context: Mapping Chiral Ring Operators The chiral ring of the planar theory is generated by a single mesonic operator, constructed by contracting the $F$ diagonal bifundamental fields along a path starting from the $U(1)_\eta$ flavor node and ending at the opposite flavor node. This operator carries trial R-charge $0$ and $U(1)_\eta$ charge $1$ . Its uniqueness follows from the F-term relations imposed by the planar superpotential $\mathcal{W}_{\text{planar}}$ .

section_title: Global Symmetries and Gauge Invariant Operators
content: Mapping Chiral Ring Operators The chiral ring of the planar theory is generated by a single mesonic operator, constructed by contracting the $F$ diagonal bifundamental fields along a path starting from the $U(1)_\eta$ flavor node and ending at the opposite flavor node. This operator carries trial R-charge $0$ and $U(1)_\eta$ charge $1$ . Its uniqueness follows from the F-term relations imposed by the planar superpotential $\mathcal{W}_{\text{planar}}$ . From the superconformal index, we expect this mesonic operator to be mapped to a monopole operator generating the chiral ring of the SQCD. The bare monopole operators $\mathfrak{M}^{\pm,0,\dots,0}$ are gauge variant, but they can be dressed to form gauge-invariant operators, as reviewed in Chapter . We now argue that the relevant dressing involves the gaugino fields. Turning on GNO fluxes induces a monopole background in which the gauge group is partially broken. For a fundamental monopole, the breaking pattern is \[ U(N)\;\longrightarrow\; U(1)\times U(N-1)\,. \] Accordingly, all gauge-variant operators decompose into representations of the residual gauge group. The branching rules for irreducible representations under $U(N)\supset U(N-1)\times U(1)$ are

These latter components can be used to dress monopole operators. Dressing the monopole with $N-1$ powers of the gaugino component transforming in the fundamental of $U(N-1)$ compensates these charges, yielding

following_context: In the theory considered here, we have $k=-\tfrac{F}{2}+N$ and $\ell=-1$ . As a result, the monopole $\mathfrak{M}^{+,0,\dots,0}$ can be dressed $N-1$ times with gaugino components in the fundamental representation of $U(N-1)$ to form a gauge-invariant operator [Footnote: For $k=\tfrac{F}{2}-N$ and $\ell=1$, instead, the gauge-invariant operator is $\mathfrak{M}^{-,0,\dots,0}$ dressed $N-1$ times with the gaugino.] . We identify this operator as the generator of the chiral ring of $U(N)$ SQCD with Chern Simons levels $\left(-\frac{F}{2}+N,-\frac{F}{2}\right)$ and $F$ fundamental chirals, and propose that it is mapped to the planar meson described above.

content: These shifts fully Higgs each gauge node to its Cartan subgroup. Most adjoint and bifundamental fields become massive and are integrated out, leaving precisely the $\mathcal{N}=2$ planar quiver shown on the right-hand side of Figure . The shifts are not Weyl-invariant. Different Weyl-related shifts correspond to distinct Coulomb branch vacua that yield identical oscillating phases. Summing over these vacua produces a factor that cancels the $|G^{(I)}|!$ in the $\mathcal{N}=4$ measure. Finally, taking the $\tau\to+\infty$ limit yields the partition function , which coincides with the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function of the planar mirror theory, up to background terms encoded in the first line of :

following_context: We now show that the flow from $\mathcal{N}=4$ to $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror dualities described in Figure is consistent with the equations of motion of the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories. In particular, we demonstrate that the vacua corresponding to the shifts in gauge fugacities satisfy both the F-term and D-term equations . Moreover, we show that fluctuations around these vacua reproduce the equations of motion of the resulting $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories [Footnote: Following the analysis of \cite{Closset_2023b}, the solutions to the equations of motion come in four possible families: \item \textbf{Higgs vacua}, where only the scalar components of the chiral multiplets acquire a VEV, and span a GLSM with a \emph{flag manifold} as the target space.

section_title: The View from the Equations of Motion
content: We now show that the flow from $\mathcal{N}=4$ to $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror dualities described in Figure is consistent with the equations of motion of the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories. In particular, we demonstrate that the vacua corresponding to the shifts in gauge fugacities satisfy both the F-term and D-term equations . Moreover, we show that fluctuations around these vacua reproduce the equations of motion of the resulting $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories [Footnote: Following the analysis of \cite{Closset_2023b}, the solutions to the equations of motion come in four possible families: \item \textbf{Higgs vacua}, where only the scalar components of the chiral multiplets acquire a VEV, and span a GLSM with a \emph{flag manifold} as the target space. \item \textbf{Topological vacua}, where the real scalars in the vector multiplet acquire a VEV giving a mass to the remaining matter content. The resulting theory in the infrared is a TQFT. \item \textbf{Hybrid vacua}, which are a mixture of the above two cases. Typically, these are products of TQFTs and GLSMs on flag manifolds. \item \textbf{Strongly coupled vacua} arise only for specific values of the CS levels. These are encountered near the origin of the classical Coulomb branch once the semiclassical approximation breaks down.] . This provides a non-trivial consistency check of our proposal.

In particular, we demonstrate that the vacua corresponding to the shifts in gauge fugacities satisfy both the F-term and D-term equations . Moreover, we show that fluctuations around these vacua reproduce the equations of motion of the resulting $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories [Footnote: Following the analysis of \cite{Closset_2023b}, the solutions to the equations of motion come in four possible families: \item \textbf{Higgs vacua}, where only the scalar components of the chiral multiplets acquire a VEV, and span a GLSM with a \emph{flag manifold} as the target space. \item \textbf{Topological vacua}, where the real scalars in the vector multiplet acquire a VEV giving a mass to the remaining matter content. The resulting theory in the infrared is a TQFT. \item \textbf{Hybrid vacua}, which are a mixture of the above two cases. Typically, these are products of TQFTs and GLSMs on flag manifolds. \item \textbf{Strongly coupled vacua} arise only for specific values of the CS levels. These are encountered near the origin of the classical Coulomb branch once the semiclassical approximation breaks down.] . This provides a non-trivial consistency check of our proposal.

content: Electric theory. We begin by considering the $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $F$ flavors. When viewed as an $\mathcal{N}=2$ theory, its field content consists of $F$ fundamental and anti-fundamental chiral multiplets $Q$ and $\tilde Q$ , together with an adjoint chiral multiplet $\Phi$ . We also turn on a real FI parameter $\eta$ . \newpage After diagonalizing the real scalar $\sigma$ in the vector multiplet, the F-term and D-term equations take the following form :

following_context: Here $Q^i_\alpha$ , $\tilde Q^\alpha_i$ , and $\Phi^\alpha_\beta$ denote the vacuum expectation values of the scalar components of the corresponding chiral multiplets. The real scalar $\sigma$ can always be diagonalized by a gauge transformation, and we write $\sigma^\beta_\alpha = \delta^\beta_\alpha \sigma_\alpha$ . The real parameters $X_i$ , $\tau$ , and $\eta$ are the real masses associated with the $SU(F)$ , $U(1)_\tau$ , and $U(1)_\eta$ global symmetries, respectively, with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^F X_i=0$ .

content: Here $Q^i_\alpha$ , $\tilde Q^\alpha_i$ , and $\Phi^\alpha_\beta$ denote the vacuum expectation values of the scalar components of the corresponding chiral multiplets. The real scalar $\sigma$ can always be diagonalized by a gauge transformation, and we write $\sigma^\beta_\alpha = \delta^\beta_\alpha \sigma_\alpha$ . The real parameters $X_i$ , $\tau$ , and $\eta$ are the real masses associated with the $SU(F)$ , $U(1)_\tau$ , and $U(1)_\eta$ global symmetries, respectively, with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^F X_i=0$ . The equations in the first line of arise from the $\mathcal{N}=4$ superpotential \[ \mathcal{W}=\sum_{i=1}^F\sum_{\alpha,\beta=1}^N Q^i_\alpha \Phi^\alpha_\beta \tilde Q^\beta_i\,, \] those in the second line correspond to real mass terms, and the last line is the one-loop corrected D-term equation.

section_title: Large-$\tau$ deformation and vacuum.
content: We now introduce a real mass deformation for the axial symmetry $U(1)_\tau$ by taking $\tau$ to be large and positive. Rather than solving the vacuum equations in full generality, we focus on vacua in which the fundamental fields remain massless. To this end, we set $X_i=0$ and take $\tau\gg 0$ . The effective mass of $Q^i_\alpha$ is $\sigma_\alpha-\tfrac{\tau}{2}$ , so we choose \[ \sigma_\alpha=\tfrac{\tau}{2}\,. \] The F-term equations then imply $\tilde Q^\alpha_i=\Phi^\alpha_\beta=0$ . In addition, we set $Q^i_\alpha=0$ so that these fields do not acquire expectation values and remain light degrees of freedom. The D-term equation reduces to

section_title: The View from the Equations of Motion
content: This reproduces the vacuum described in . The resulting light degrees of freedom, determined from , coincide with those obtained from the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function analysis. Expanding the equations of motion around this vacuum also reproduces the correct self and mixed Chern Simons interactions of the planar theory in Figure . The planar superpotential arises from the surviving F-term constraints, while the monopole superpotential is not directly accessible from this analysis. As a technical aside, we comment on the similarities and differences between the analysis based on the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function and that based on the equations of motion. It can be verified that, under a real mass deformation parametrized by $\tau$ , the requirement that the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function factorizes into a divergent oscillating phase times a finite integral is equivalent to solving the D-term equations up to $\mathcal{O}(\tau^0)$ . This correspondence arises because large shifts of the gauge fugacities in the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function precisely capture motion far along the Coulomb branch of the moduli space. By contrast, the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function is insensitive to motion along the Higgs branch. As a consequence, solving the remaining equations of motion provides an independent and non-trivial check of the consistency of the real mass deformation considered above. Finally, when multiple vacua are present, the analysis of the equations of motion alone does not yield a systematic prescription for identifying how vacua are mapped across the duality. In this context, the divergent phase extracted from the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function plays a crucial role: it is sensitive to the spectrum of massive fields in a given vacuum and therefore provides a powerful constraint on the correct matching of vacua under the duality.

section_title: Shifting \texorpdfstring{$\ell$ via Witten's $SL(2;\mathbb Z)$ Action}{l}
content: In this chapter, we will primarily focus on dualities involving unitary gauge theories of the form $U(N)_{(k,k-N)}$ , namely theories in which the abelian Chern Simons level $\ell$ is fixed to $\ell=-1$ . Nevertheless, it is often useful to relate these theories to their special-unitary counterparts. In particular, an $SU(N)_k$ Chern Simons theory can be obtained from a $U(N)$ theory by removing the diagonal $U(1)$ factor. This is achieved by introducing a background BF coupling between the topological symmetry $U(1)_Y$ and a new abelian symmetry $U(1)_B$ , and subsequently gauging the topological symmetry. This procedure is commonly referred to as the \emph{ungauging} of the diagonal $U(1)\subset U(N)$ . Within the conventions adopted throughout this thesis, the ungauging operation is implemented as where the double circle denotes an $SU(N)$ gauge node. In the $U(N)$ SQCD description the non-abelian flavor symmetry is $SU(F)$ , and therefore the real mass parameters satisfy $\sum_{j=1}^F X_j = 0$ . The parameter $\xi$ represents a possible background Chern Simons coupling for the flavor symmetry. After ungauging, the resulting $SU(N)$ theory exhibits a baryonic $U(1)_B$ symmetry, and the real masses obey $\sum_{j=1}^F \widetilde{X}_j = F B$ . \newpage Conversely, starting from an $SU(N)$ SQCD theory, one can recover a unitary gauge group by gauging the baryonic symmetry $U(1)_B$ . This inverse operation is implemented as where $\xi_1$ and $\xi_2$ denote possible background Chern Simons levels for the $U(F)$ global symmetry of the SQCD on the right-hand side, and the term $-\Delta\eta \tfrac{N}{F}$ corresponds to a background FI coupling. One may verify explicitly that applying the ungauging operation followed by the gauging operation maps a theory back to itself, as expected. In what follows, we will make use of the ungauging operation to derive the $SU(N)$ version of the chiral planar mirror duality for SQCD, starting from the unitary duality presented in Figure . On the electric side, this procedure yields $\mathcal{N}=2$ $SU(N)_{-\frac{F}{2}+N}$ SQCD. On the mirror side, the topological symmetry of the unitary theory is realized as a flavor symmetry acting on the bottom flavor node of the planar quiver. Consequently, the ungauging operation described above translates into gauging this flavor node. To implement this gauging consistently, it is essential to keep track of the background Chern Simons and BF terms that are not explicitly shown in Figure , but are instead encoded in the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function . Taking these contributions into account, one arrives at the chiral planar mirror duality depicted in Figure . This duality was first presented in [Footnote: The newly introduced gauge node is a $U(1)_{\frac{1}{2}}$ theory with a single chiral multiplet and therefore undergoes confinement. As a result, one obtains a chiral field that is neutral under all gauge symmetries and is coupled to the theory as a flipper for a gauge-invariant monopole operator. This does not lead to a cascade of sequential confinements, except in the special case $N=2$.] . By gauging the baryonic symmetry in Figure , following the prescription in , we recover the mirror duality for $U(N)_{(k,k+N)}$ SQCD. At this stage, one may further include a Chern Simons term at level $\Delta \ell$ for the baryonic symmetry before gauging. This results in a $U(N)$ gauge theory with CS levels $(k,k+N(1+\Delta \ell))$ . The combined effect of gauging and ungauging operations, together with the introduction of background Chern Simons terms, realizes Witten’s $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ action on the topological symmetry of the $U(N)$ theory. On the mirror side, this procedure introduces an additional $U(1)_{\Delta \ell}$ gauge node, which is coupled to the rest of the planar quiver through a BF interaction. The resulting chiral planar mirror duality is shown in Figure . For $\Delta \ell = 0,\pm 1$ , the planar quiver in Figure can be further simplified by explicitly integrating out the additional gauge node. When $\Delta \ell = 0$ , the path integral over the extra node produces a delta function constraint. This constraint fixes the gauge field of the node immediately to its right, which is therefore demoted to a flavor node. As a result, one recovers the original planar mirror dual of $U(N)_{(-\frac{F}{2}+N,-\frac{F}{2})}$ SQCD. For $\Delta \ell = \pm 1$ , the additional gauge node is a topological $U(1)_{\pm 1}$ sector. This theory is almost trivial, and its path integral can be performed exactly using standard results for abelian Chern Simons theories . Diagrammatically, this integration corresponds to In particular, for $\Delta \ell = 1$ we obtain a planar abelian dual of $U(N)_{(k,k)}$ SQCD. Integrating out the additional $U(1)_{1}$ node shifts the Chern Simons level of the adjacent gauge node by $-1$ . As a consequence, the bottom-most node becomes a $U(1)_{-1/2}$ gauge theory with a single fundamental chiral multiplet. This sector can be locally dualized into a free chiral field. Planar duals of $U(N)_{(k,k)}$ will play a key role in the study of massive deformations of these theories (Chapter ), while planar duals of $SU(N)_k$ will serve as the starting point of the analysis of non-supersymmetric dualities (Chapter ). \newpage

following_context: In this section, we focus on applying our findings to more general linear quiver gauge theories. To that end, we pick the example of the $FT[U(N)]$ quiver gauge theory as a starting point. This theory is closely related to the well-known $T[SU(N)]$ theory introduced in , differing only by the presence of an additional singlet chiral field transforming in the adjoint representation of a global $SU(N)$ symmetry and flipping the Higgs-branch moment map.

section_title: Planar Abelian Duals of \texorpdfstring{$A_N$}{AN} Quiver Gauge Theories
content: In this section, we focus on applying our findings to more general linear quiver gauge theories. To that end, we pick the example of the $FT[U(N)]$ quiver gauge theory as a starting point. This theory is closely related to the well-known $T[SU(N)]$ theory introduced in , differing only by the presence of an additional singlet chiral field transforming in the adjoint representation of a global $SU(N)$ symmetry and flipping the Higgs-branch moment map. The $FT[U(N)]$ theory plays a distinguished role in our analysis because it is \emph{self-mirror} , a property that will be central to what follows. We will make these ideas more precise below. The $FT[U(N)]$ theory is a strongly interacting SCFT with global symmetry ${\color{black}{SU(N)_{\vec{X}}}}\times {\color{black}{SU(N)_{\vec{Y}}}}\times U(1)_{\tau}$ [Footnote: Notice that we work in an off-shell parametrization wherein the manifest symmetry is $U(N)$ and not $SU(N)$, as in \cite{Benvenuti:2023qtv,Benvenuti:2024mpn}.] . We denote this theory compactly: where $\vec{X}$ , $\vec{Y}$ are fugacities parametrizing the global symmetries and the label $-$ " is included for later convenience (see Section ). The operator spectrum of the $FT[U(N)]$ SCFT contains two operators, denoted $A_{\vec{X}}$ and $A_{\vec{Y}}$ , transforming in the adjoint representations of $SU(N)_{\vec{X}}$ and $SU(N)_{\vec{Y}}$ , respectively. These operators should be interpreted as the moment maps associated with the two non-Abelian global symmetry factors. The theory admits a Lagrangian UV completion in the form of a linear quiver gauge theory, shown in Figure . In this description, the UV global symmetry is

section_title: Planar Abelian Duals of \texorpdfstring{$A_N$}{AN} Quiver Gauge Theories
content: In this UV completion, the operator $A_{\vec{X}}$ is realized perturbatively as the traceless adjoint singlet field $a_N$ associated with the last node of the quiver. By contrast, the operator $A_{\vec{Y}}$ emerges non-perturbatively. It is constructed from a collection of gauge-invariant monopole operators with various GNO charges, together with the traces of the adjoint chiral fields associated with the gauge nodes. More precisely, the off-diagonal components of $A_{\vec{Y}}$ are identified with monopole operators carrying magnetic fluxes of the form $\mathfrak{M}^{+0\dots}, \mathfrak{M}^{++0\dots}, \dots, \mathfrak{M}^{0+0\dots},\mathfrak{M}^{0++0\dots}, \dots$ , together with their adjoint (with negative GNO charges), and the $N-1$ traces $\text{Tr}(a_{i})$ with $i=1,\dots,N-1$ . For example, for $N=4$ we have:

content: The $FT[U(N)]$ quiver gauge theory is self-mirror under the exchange of the manifest and topological symmetries. We also indicate the assignment of trial R-charges for the bifundamental and adjoint chiral fields. Notice that the bifundamental fields have trial R-charge $r$ and adjoint chiral fields have trial R-charge $2-2r$ on both sides of the duality. This is a special feature of the $FT[U(N)]$ theory. As a consequence, the two moment maps $A_{\vec{X}}, A_{\vec{Y}}$ have both trial R-charge $2-r$.

content: where $\mathcal{D}_i$ are the traceless diagonal generators of $SU(N)_{\vec{Y}}$ . The $FT[U(N)]$ admits a second UV completion, as shown in Figure . In this alternative description, the roles of the two $SU(N)$ global symmetries are exchanged. In each completion, only one of the two global $SU(N)$ symmetries is realized manifestly as the flavor symmetry rotating the last bifundamental. The second $SU(N)$ global symmetry instead enhances from the $N-1$ topological symmetries due to all the nodes being balanced. The two UV completions are related by mirror duality - in this case, these theories are self-mirror since they describe the same IR fixed point. This self-mirror property is reflected in the structure of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function. The partition function of the first UV completion can be defined recursively as:

In the remainder of this work, we will treat the global symmetry as $U(N)_{\vec{X}}\times U(N)_{\vec{Y}}$ rather than strictly imposing the tracelessness constraints $\sum_i X_i=\sum_i Y_i=0$ . In practice, this corresponds to relaxing these constraints in the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function and does not affect any of the physical conclusions.

section_title: \texorpdfstring{The Flow to $\mathcal{N}=2$: The $G[U(N)]$ Theory}{The Flow to N=2}
content: Starting from the two UV completions of self-mirror $FT[U(N)]$ quiver gauge theory (top row), we study the effect of adding a real mass deformation that breaks the $U(1)_{\tau}$ global symmetry. The resulting duality relates a chiral, non-Abelian, linear quiver gauge theory to a planar, Abelian quiver gauge theory. The details of the global symmetries and Chern-Simons interactions are expanded upon in the text.
raw_latex: \caption{Starting from the two UV completions of self-mirror $FT[U(N)]$ quiver gauge theory (top row), we study the effect of adding a real mass deformation that breaks the $U(1)_{\tau}$ global symmetry. The resulting duality relates a chiral, non-Abelian, linear quiver gauge theory to a planar, Abelian quiver gauge theory. The details of the global symmetries and Chern-Simons interactions are expanded upon in the text.}
following_context: Starting from the self-mirror pair relating the two UV completions of the $FT[U(N)]$ SCFT in Figure , we will now consider a real mass deformation that produces a new duality relating a chiral quiver to a planar quiver. The resulting RG flow is depicted in Figure . As in the previous section, the real mass deformation on the electric side is chosen to flow to a chiral, non-Abelian quiver gauge theory.

section_title: \texorpdfstring{The Flow to $\mathcal{N}=2$: The $G[U(N)]$ Theory}{The Flow to N=2}
content: Starting from the self-mirror pair relating the two UV completions of the $FT[U(N)]$ SCFT in Figure , we will now consider a real mass deformation that produces a new duality relating a chiral quiver to a planar quiver. The resulting RG flow is depicted in Figure . As in the previous section, the real mass deformation on the electric side is chosen to flow to a chiral, non-Abelian quiver gauge theory. On the mirror side, matching the same vacuum requires moving onto a point of the Coulomb branch where all gauge symmetries are broken to their maximal torus. As a result, the IR description on the dual side is given by a planar Abelian quiver. The two theories appearing at the bottom of Figure are IR dual to one another and can be regarded as two distinct UV completions of a single interacting SCFT, which we denote by $G[U(N)]$ [Footnote: Named so after Gabriel Pedde Ungureanu at the time, the author did not realise how important these theories were and how much confusion could be avoided if he had just called them an $\mathcal S$-wall like their $\mathcal{N}=4$ counterparts.] . This theory has global symmetry $SU(N)\times U(1)^{N-1}$ and will play an important role in the subsequent analysis. We represent it compactly as \tikzstyle flavor =[rectangle,draw=red!50,thick,inner sep = 0pt, minimum size = 6mm] \tikzstyle manifest =[rectangle,draw=blue!50,thick,inner sep = 0pt, minimum size = 6mm] \tikzstyle gauge =[circle,draw=black!50,thick,inner sep = 0pt, minimum size = 6mm] \tikzset -<-/.style= decoration= markings, mark=at position .5 with \arrow < ,postaction= decorate \tikzset ->-/.style= decoration= markings, mark=at position .5 with \arrow < ,postaction= decorate We emphasize that, in our notation, the first argument of $G[\cdot\,;\cdot]$ corresponds to fugacities for the $SU(N)$ global symmetry, while the second argument parametrizes the $U(1)^{N-1}$ global symmetry. We now describe this deformation in detail, starting from the first UV completion of the $FT[U(N)]$ theory, which we refer to as the electric description, and in which the $U(N)_{\vec{X}}$ global symmetry is manifest using the machinery of the $\mathbf{S^3_b}$ partition function.. We implement a real mass deformation by introducing a large background expectation value for the scalar in the $U(1)_\tau$ vector multiplet. At the level of fugacities in the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function, this deformation is realized as

content: and subsequently taking the limit $\tau \to +\infty$ . Here $\omega^{(J)}_{\alpha}$ denotes the $\alpha$ -th Cartan fugacity of the $J$ -th gauge group in the quiver, ordered from left to right. This deformation completely breaks the non-Abelian gauge symmetry to its maximal torus, allowing the massive fields to be consistently integrated out. After integrating out the massive degrees of freedom, the theory flows in the infrared to an $\mathcal{N}=2$ planar Abelian quiver gauge theory, shown in Figure . This planar quiver is IR dual to the chiral quiver depicted in Figure , and together they furnish two distinct UV completions of the same interacting SCFT, which we denote by $G[U(N)]$ . \newpage A few comments regarding the structure of the superpotential and Chern Simons interactions of the planar quiver in Figure are in order: \item For every closed triangular cycle in the planar quiver, there is a cubic superpotential term. These interactions descend directly from the cubic $\mathcal{N}=4$ superpotential terms that are preserved by the real mass deformation. We collectively denote these contributions by $\mathcal{W}_{\text{planar}}$ . The assignment of trial R-charges is as follows: diagonal chiral fields are assigned R-charge $r$ , while vertical chiral fields are assigned R-charge $2-r$ . This assignment is consistent with the cubic planar superpotential. Moreover, the FI parameters depend explicitly on the value of $r$ , reflecting a mixing between the topological symmetries and the R-symmetry. \item Due to the adjoint Higgsing of the gauge nodes, monopole operators appear in the effective description. In particular, there is a linear monopole superpotential term for each monopole carrying charges $+1$ and $-1$ under the upper and lower gauge nodes connected by a vertical edge . We denote these contributions collectively by $\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}$ . These monopole superpotential terms play a crucial role in the infrared dynamics: they are essential for the enhancement of the topological $U(1)^{N-1}$ symmetry to the non-Abelian $SU(N)$ global symmetry, as will be discussed later. \item The planar theory also includes both self and mixed Chern-Simons interactions. Each gauge node carries a Chern-Simons level $+1$ . In addition, for each pair of gauge nodes connected by a diagonal (respectively vertical) chiral multiplet, there is a mixed Chern-Simons interaction with level $-1$ (respectively $+1$ ). All such Chern-Simons terms arise from integrating out fermionic modes that become massive along the real mass deformation. \item Finally, there are shifts in the FI parameters induced by the mixing between the topological symmetries of the gauge nodes and the R-symmetry. The FI parameters for the gauge nodes in the $J$ -th column from the right take the form

content: Mapping Operators and Global Symmetries

Moreover, we find that the residual phases also agree, As a consequence, after removing the universal phase factor in the $\tau \to +\infty$ limit, we conclude that the partition functions of the two UV completions coincide:
following_context: We now comment on the duality map between global symmetries and the chiral rings of the two theories. Let us begin with the \textit{global symmetries} . As stated at the beginning of this section, the $G[U(N)]$ SCFT has a $U(1)^{N-1} \times SU(N)$ global symmetry.

section_title: Mapping Operators and Global Symmetries
content: We now comment on the duality map between global symmetries and the chiral rings of the two theories. Let us begin with the \textit{global symmetries} . As stated at the beginning of this section, the $G[U(N)]$ SCFT has a $U(1)^{N-1} \times SU(N)$ global symmetry. In the chiral UV completion (Figure ), both factors of this symmetry are realized manifestly in the ultraviolet: the $SU(N)$ symmetry appears as a flavor symmetry acting on the matter fields, while the $U(1)^{N-1}$ symmetries correspond to the topological symmetries associated with the $N-1$ gauge nodes. In contrast, in the planar UV completion shown in Figure , the roles of these symmetries are exchanged. The $U(1)^{N-1}$ symmetry is realized as a flavor symmetry that is preserved by the planar superpotential, up to gauge transformations. The $SU(N)$ global symmetry, on the other hand, emerges in the infrared from the enhancement of the topological symmetries associated with the gauge nodes. We recall that the presence of the monopole superpotential $\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}$ plays a crucial role in this enhancement mechanism. In particular, $\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}$ breaks the $U(1)^k$ topological symmetries associated with a column containing $k$ gauge nodes down to a single diagonal $U(1)$ . As a result, each column of gauge nodes effectively contributes one independent topological $U(1)$ symmetry. The enhancement of these remaining topological symmetries in the infrared gives rise to the non-Abelian $SU(N)$ global symmetry of the $G[U(N)]$ SCFT. Let us now move to the \textit{operator map} . The chiral ring of the planar $G[U(N)]$ theory shown in Figure is generated by the $N-1$ vertical bifundamental chiral fields connecting adjacent vertical flavor nodes. Up to F-term relations imposed by the planar superpotential $\mathcal{W}_{\text{planar}}$ , these fields can be taken as the only independent generators of the chiral ring. We expect these operators to map, under the duality, to monopole operators in the chiral $G[U(N)]$ theory in Figure . This expectation is correct, with the important caveat that the fundamental monopole operators are gauge variant and must be appropriately dressed by powers of fundamental chiral multiplets in order to construct gauge-invariant operators. To illustrate this, let us focus on a local segment of the chiral $G[U(N)]$ quiver, \tikzset ->-/.style= decoration= markings, mark=at position .5 with \arrow > ,postaction= decorate \tikzset -<-/.style= decoration= markings, mark=at position .5 with \arrow < ,postaction= decorate where the $k$ -th gauge node is a $U(k)$ gauge group. The fundamental monopole operator associated with this node carries non-trivial gauge charge and therefore must be dressed by $(k-1)$ powers of the bifundamental field $Q$ in order to form a gauge-invariant operator. In this way, one constructs $N-1$ dressed fundamental monopole operators, one for each gauge node of the chiral $G[U(N)]$ theory. These dressed monopoles generate the chiral ring of the theory. We now verify that these operators have the correct quantum numbers to be mapped to the $N-1$ vertical bifundamental chiral fields connecting adjacent vertical flavor nodes in the planar mirror theory. This identification can also be independently checked using the superconformal index. Assume that each bifundamental chiral multiplet in the chiral theory (such as $Q$ , $P$ , and so on) has trial R-charge $r$ . Consequently, the vertical bifundamental chiral fields in the planar mirror theory, which are mapped to these monopole operators, have R-charge $2-2r$ . Using the general monopole R-charge formula , the R-charge of the dressed monopole operator associated with the $k$ -th gauge node, \[ \mathrm{tr}\!\left(Q^{k-1}\,\mathfrak{M}^{0,\dots,0,-,0,\dots,0}\right), \] is given by

following_context: The first term arises from the mixing between the topological symmetry and the R-symmetry [Footnote: This mixing can be read off from the coefficient of $\frac{iQ}{2}$ in the FI parameter of the $k$-th gauge node, $Y_k - Y_{k+1} + \frac{iQ}{2}(r-1)$. The contribution to the R-charge is obtained by multiplying this coefficient by the monopole’s topological charge.] . The second and third terms correspond to the contributions of the bifundamental fermions, the term $-(k-1)$ arises from the gaugini, and the final term $(k-1)r$ accounts for the dressing by $Q^{k-1}$ .

section_title: Mapping Operators and Global Symmetries
content: The first term arises from the mixing between the topological symmetry and the R-symmetry [Footnote: This mixing can be read off from the coefficient of $\frac{iQ}{2}$ in the FI parameter of the $k$-th gauge node, $Y_k - Y_{k+1} + \frac{iQ}{2}(r-1)$. The contribution to the R-charge is obtained by multiplying this coefficient by the monopole’s topological charge.] . The second and third terms correspond to the contributions of the bifundamental fermions, the term $-(k-1)$ arises from the gaugini, and the final term $(k-1)r$ accounts for the dressing by $Q^{k-1}$ . We thus conclude that the $N-1$ dressed fundamental monopole operators of the chiral $G[U(N)]$ theory map precisely to the $N-1$ vertical bifundamental chiral fields connecting adjacent vertical flavor nodes in the planar mirror theory. In the remainder of this chapter, we denote these gauge-invariant dressed monopole operators collectively as $\mathfrak{M}_d$ , omitting the explicit dressing fields for notational simplicity. Thus, we see that longer quiver theories also admit planar mirror dual descriptions.

On the other hand, the choice of CS levels \textbf{is not arbitrary} and is fixed by the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ theory: each massive (anti)fundamental multiple contributes as $(-\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2})$ to the CS level and the massive adjoint chiral contributes as $(N,0)$ . In particular, we always have $\ell=-1$ .

section_title: Towards an Algorithmic Approach
content: The number of (anti-) fundamentals $n_f$ and $n_a$ satisfy $F=n_f + n_a$ but are otherwise unconstrained and depend on the specific mass deformation that we consider. On the other hand, the choice of CS levels \textbf{is not arbitrary} and is fixed by the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ theory: each massive (anti)fundamental multiple contributes as $(-\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2})$ to the CS level and the massive adjoint chiral contributes as $(N,0)$ . In particular, we always have $\ell=-1$ . These features persist in all non-Abelian linear quiver theories analyzed in this chapter. One can then consider the same limit on the mirror side and try to identify the dual vacuum. In general, this is a non-trivial exercise as there are multiple potential vacua corresponding to possible ways of moving on the Coulomb branch of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror (which, in turn, match how we shift gauge fugacities in the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function). More generally, one can start on the electric side with a linear unitary $\mathcal{N}=4$ quiver with gauge group $\prod_{N_i} U(N_i)$ . We consider a real mass deformation, paired with a VEV for the scalar in the $\mathcal{N}=2$ vector multiplets, such that the adjoint chiral fields and one $\mathcal{N}=2$ chiral multiplet in each matter hypermultiplet acquire a positive real mass. Integrating out the massive field we obtain a chiral linear quiver: \tikzstyle flavor =[rectangle,draw=red!50,thick,inner sep = 0pt, minimum size = 6mm] where the CS levels $k_i$ are fixed by the parent theory:

section_title: Towards an Algorithmic Approach
content: As before, one can consider the mirror dual quiver theory and implement the same limit to reach the dual vacuum. This remains a non-trivial exercise. Starting from the equality of the partition functions of pairs of $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror theories is a hugely successful, albeit tedious, paradigm to find $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror duals. Clearly, it is desirable to have a more systematic way to determine the mirror dual of a generic flavored $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver without the need to resort to $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition functions. We do so by adapting the idea of the local dualization algorithm of in this section.

section_title: Lightning Recap: The \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$}{N=4} Dualization Algorithm
content: The dualization algorithm for $\mathcal{N}=4$ linear quiver theories provides a purely field-theoretical proof of mirror duality, assuming only basic Seiberg-like dualities, and allows us to access the full map of symmetries and protected gauge invariant operators. It is well known that $\mathcal{N}=4$ linear quiver theories can be realized on Hanany-Witten brane set-ups and inherit mirror dualities from the $\mathcal{S}$ -duality of Type IIB set-ups . The idea of the algorithm originates from the observation that on linear or circular brane setups, $\mathcal{S}-$ duality can act locally on each 5-brane creating an $\mathcal{S}-$ duality wall on its right and an $\mathcal{S}^{-1}$ -duality wall on its left: $D5= \mathcal{S}\!\cdot\! NS5 \!\cdot\! \mathcal{S}^{-1}$ and $\overline{NS5}= \mathcal{S} \!\cdot\! D5 \!\cdot\! \mathcal{S}^{-1}.$ The dualization algorithm implements this local action of $\mathcal{S}$ -duality in field theory. The dualization algorithm is based on two fundamental ingredients: \item a realization of the generator $\mathcal{S}$ of the duality group $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ that satisfies the fusion to identity $\mathcal{S}\mathcal{S}^{-1} = \mathbb{I}$ . For $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories, this can be defined from the $FT[U(N)]$ SCFT : As discussed in Section , the $FT[U(N)]$ theory admits a Lagrangian UV completion (Figure ) and is self-mirror under the exchange of emergent and manifest $SU(N)$ symmetries. The $\mathcal{S}$ -wall also satisfies the fusion to identity encoded in a partition function identity:

preceding_context: The dualization algorithm is based on two fundamental ingredients: \item a realization of the generator $\mathcal{S}$ of the duality group $SL(2;\mathbb{Z})$ that satisfies the fusion to identity $\mathcal{S}\mathcal{S}^{-1} = \mathbb{I}$ . section_title: Lightning Recap: The \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$}{N=4} Dualization Algorithm
content: Applying the $\mathcal{N}=4$ dualization to $U(N)$ SQCD with $[F+2N, F+2N]$ flavors proceeds as follows - in the first line, the quiver is broken into its constituent QFT blocks and each block is sandwiched between an $\mathcal{S}^{-1}$ and $\mathcal{S}$-wall. We have explicitly kept the integral and integrating measures to remind the reader that care must be taken when the gauge nodes are ungauged to implement the algorithm. The resulting QFT blocks obtained after the local action are written in the second line, and are glued back together by exploiting the fusion to identity property of the $\mathcal{S}$-wall to obtain the final theory in the third line. At this stage, an explicit Lagrangian description may be written using the UV completion of the $FT[U(N)]$ theory, which is indeed the mirror description one would get by utilizing conventional HW brane setups. Note that we would need to incorporate HW transitions to dualize more general quiver theories.

section_title: Lightning Recap: The \texorpdfstring{$\mathcal{N}=4$}{N=4} Dualization Algorithm
content: We point out that this move is slightly different than naively sandwiching the previous move between an $\mathcal{S}^{-1}$ and $\mathcal{S}$ wall. Observe that the gluing on the RHS \textbf{does not} include the addition of $U(N)$ adjoint multiplets coupling linearly to the moment map of the glued $FT[U(N)]$ theory. We consider the case of $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $F+2N$ flavors to demonstrate the algorithm at work (Figure ). When decomposing the theory into QFT blocks, it is important to keep in mind that each block effectively plays the role of a matrix element, with two sets of indices corresponding to the two $N$ -dimensional vectors of parameters associated with the two $U(N)$ global symmetries. In particular, note that flavor blocks are accompanied by an identity wall, reflecting this matrix structure. It was shown that, armed with the fundamental blocks and duality moves described above, one can construct the mirror of a vast set of $\mathcal{N}=4$ Lagrangian theories . In , the local dualization algorithm paradigm has been extended to a class of generalized $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver theories that can be realized in set-ups involving $NS, NS', D5, D5$ branes preserving four supercharges. Unlike the $\mathcal{N}=4$ case, where brane setups directly inform the structure of the low-energy field theory, here the situation is reversed: the algorithmic field theory approach yields an exact dual description, while the corresponding brane configurations are difficult to interpret directly. This inversion of logic highlights the power of the algorithmic method — not only does it provide precise duals, but it also offers a new lens through which to understand and possibly reconstruct the brane setups themselves. Motivated by this, our aim is now to develop a similar algorithm tailored to chiral-planar $\mathcal{N}=2$ theories in the remainder of this section.

To that end, we consider real mass deformations at the level of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ fusion to identity (Equation ).

section_title: The Algorithm At Work
content: We are finally ready to define a dualization algorithm to dualize $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver gauge theories. Along the lines of , we define the steps underlying the algorithmic dualization of an $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver: \item \textbf{Step 1:} The quiver is chopped into its basic QFT blocks that can be either bifundamentals or (anti-)fundamental chiral fields. This step is performed by freezing the gauge interactions that will be reintroduced later. \item \textbf{Step 2:} Each QFT block is dualized via the basic duality moves described. These identities, or \textit{basic moves} are summarized for reference in Table . \item \textbf{Step 3:} The dualized blocks are glued together via the $\mathcal{N}=2$ chiral/planar fusion to identity when the original gauging is restored. Each of these steps will be illustrated in more detail in the following sections with explicit examples. We reiterate that the choice of CS levels is \textbf{not arbitrary} - it is completely fixed by our $\mathcal{N}=4$ parent. We will use the algorithm to dualize non-Abelian chiral $\mathcal{N}=2$ quivers into abelian-planar quivers. Although the same algorithm can be applied in reverse — starting from a planar abelian quiver to obtain its chiral dual — we do not discuss this case here. \newpage \newpage

Although the same algorithm can be applied in reverse — starting from a planar abelian quiver to obtain its chiral dual — we do not discuss this case here. \newpage \newpage

following_context: As a first example of the application of the algorithm, we consider the case of $U(N)_{(-\frac{F}{2}+N,-\frac{F}{2})}$ SQCD with $[n_f,n_a]$ flavors, constrained by the relation $F=n_f+n_a$ . As we had mentioned in Section , these theories can be obtained from $\mathcal{N}=4$ $U(N)$ SQCD with $F$ flavors by a positive real axial mass deformation at the level of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function. However, instead of a brute force approach, we shall do this by using the algorithmic prescription.

following_context: We start from the first case in and consider $U(N)_{(-\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}, -\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}-N)}$ SQCD with $[F_1+N, F_2+N]$ chiral multiplets: To apply our algorithm, we need to define this theory using the basic ingredients defined in Section : the fundamental blocks can be used to account for the matter content, and the gluing rules account for the gauging. The matter content of can be accounted for by considering two chiral bifundamental blocks , $F_1$ chiral fundamental blocks and $F_2$ chiral antifundamental blocks. Each (anti)fundamental block includes an identity operator $\mathbb{I}^{[N]}$ which has the effect of identifying two $U(N)$ gauge groups.

section_title: \texorpdfstring{$U(N)$ CS-SQCD with $[F_1+N, F_2+N]$ Chiral Multiplets}{U(N) with [F1+N,F2+N]}
content: We start from the first case in and consider $U(N)_{(-\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}, -\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}-N)}$ SQCD with $[F_1+N, F_2+N]$ chiral multiplets: To apply our algorithm, we need to define this theory using the basic ingredients defined in Section : the fundamental blocks can be used to account for the matter content, and the gluing rules account for the gauging. The matter content of can be accounted for by considering two chiral bifundamental blocks , $F_1$ chiral fundamental blocks and $F_2$ chiral antifundamental blocks. Each (anti)fundamental block includes an identity operator $\mathbb{I}^{[N]}$ which has the effect of identifying two $U(N)$ gauge groups. Therefore, in order to engineer the SQCD theory, where there is one $U(N)$ gauge group, we need $F_1+F_2+1$ gluing operations. These ingredients can be combined by joining the matter blocks and gluing two adjacent blocks through one of their $U(N)$ symmetries with the glue operation. Schematically the SQCD theory can be written as: In this schematic picture, multiplication between two consecutive blocks is understood. Notice the presence of the chiral measure $\Delta^{[N]}$ defined in : as we ungauge the $U(N)$ gauge symmetry and decompose the theory into fundamental QFT blocks, it becomes essential to keep track of the integration measures to recover the original theory ( ). We have suppressed global fugacity labels for brevity. One can verify that we do recover the correct CS levels for the SQCD theories upon gauging. Recall that each factor of $\Delta^{[N]}$ contributes as $(N,0)$ to the CS level. Each flavor block consists of a (anti-)fundamental chiral field, with a CS level $(-\tfrac{1}{2},-\tfrac{1}{2})$ , and an identity wall, contributing as $(N,0)$ , for a total contribution of $(-N-\tfrac{1}{2},-\tfrac{1}{2})$ . Lastly, a bifundamental chiral contributes with a CS level of $(-\tfrac{N}{2},-\tfrac{N}{2})$ . Therefore, the total CS level is:

section_title: \texorpdfstring{$U(N)$ CS-SQCD with $[F_1+N, F_2+N]$ Chiral Multiplets}{U(N) with [F1+N,F2+N]}
content: which matches the CS level of the SQCD theory we are considering. Now we dualize each block by exploiting the basic moves derived in Sections and we obtain: Note, once again, the presence of the chiral measure. The presence of the chiral measure is needed for diagonal gauging of adjacent $U(N)$ nodes to obtain connected generalized quivers. Once adjacent $U(N)$ nodes are gauged, we can dualize those subquivers by using the fusion to identity to obtain an identity wall: where each of the $U(1)$ gauge nodes on the left, from top to bottom, is identified with the corresponding $U(1)$ gauge node on the right, from top to bottom, as dictated by the fusion to identity rule. The resulting generalized quiver is shown below: The generalized quiver in can finally be written in the explicit Lagrangian form by using the planar UV completion of the $\mathcal{S}$ -walls and the definition of the planar bifundamental and fundamental blocks. The result is depicted in Figure . We now comment on several features of the duality shown in Figure . The global symmetry of the SQCD theory is, in general, \[ S\!\left[ U(F_1+N)\times U(F_2+N) \right]\times U(1), \] where the first factor is the flavor symmetry acting on the fundamental and anti-fundamental fields, while the second factor is the topological symmetry. [Footnote: In the special case \(F_1=F_2=0\), the topological symmetry enhances to \(SU(2)\). This enhancement can also be understood by considering the Aharony-like dual of the theory \cite{Amariti:2021snj}, which is Abelian, and then taking its mirror dual \cite{Tong:2000ky}.] In the planar mirror dual, after taking into account gauge transformations and superpotential constraints, the only manifest flavor symmetry is a single \(U(1)\) . The monopole superpotential \(\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}\) contains terms that are linear in monopole operators carrying magnetic flux \(-1\) and \(+1\) under pairs of gauge nodes connected by vertical links. These terms explicitly break the individual topological symmetries associated with the nodes in each column down to a single diagonal \(U(1)\) . As a result, the UV topological symmetry of the planar quiver is \[ U(1)^{F_1+F_2+N-1}, \] which enhances in the IR to \[ S\!\left[ U(F_1+N)\times U(F_2+N) \right]. \] This enhancement ensures that the global symmetry of the mirror quiver matches that of the SQCD theory. In the SQCD theory, the chiral ring is generated by mesonic operators constructed from the fundamental fields \(Q_i\) and anti-fundamentals \(\tilde Q_j\) , which transform in the bifundamental representation of \[ SU(F_1+N)\times SU(F_2+N). \] Under the duality, these mesons are mapped to gauge-invariant monopole operators in the planar Abelian theory. For instance, the meson \(Q_{F_1+N}\tilde Q_{F_2+N}\) is mapped to the monopole operator with magnetic flux \(+1\) under the topmost gauge node of the Abelian quiver. More generally, the remaining mesons are mapped to monopole operators characterized by two strings of \(+1\) magnetic fluxes, originating at the topmost node and propagating along the two upper diagonals of the planar quiver. There are precisely \((F_1+N)(F_2+N)\) such monopole operators, reproducing the bifundamental representation of the enhanced \(SU(F_1+N)\times SU(F_2+N)\) global symmetry. Finally, we note that in this class of dualities, the planar Abelian theory does not exhibit any mesonic chiral ring generators. This implies that, for all values of \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) , the SQCD theory does not admit dressed gauge-invariant monopole operators that would parametrize a non-compact moduli space.

The resulting generalized quiver is shown below: The generalized quiver in can finally be written in the explicit Lagrangian form by using the planar UV completion of the $\mathcal{S}$ -walls and the definition of the planar bifundamental and fundamental blocks. The result is depicted in Figure . We now comment on several features of the duality shown in Figure . The global symmetry of the SQCD theory is, in general, \[ S\!\left[ U(F_1+N)\times U(F_2+N) \right]\times U(1), \] where the first factor is the flavor symmetry acting on the fundamental and anti-fundamental fields, while the second factor is the topological symmetry. [Footnote: In the special case \(F_1=F_2=0\), the topological symmetry enhances to \(SU(2)\). This enhancement can also be understood by considering the Aharony-like dual of the theory \cite{Amariti:2021snj}, which is Abelian, and then taking its mirror dual \cite{Tong:2000ky}.] In the planar mirror dual, after taking into account gauge transformations and superpotential constraints, the only manifest flavor symmetry is a single \(U(1)\) . The monopole superpotential \(\mathcal{W}_{\text{monopole}}\) contains terms that are linear in monopole operators carrying magnetic flux \(-1\) and \(+1\) under pairs of gauge nodes connected by vertical links. These terms explicitly break the individual topological symmetries associated with the nodes in each column down to a single diagonal \(U(1)\) . As a result, the UV topological symmetry of the planar quiver is \[ U(1)^{F_1+F_2+N-1}, \] which enhances in the IR to \[ S\!\left[ U(F_1+N)\times U(F_2+N) \right]. \] This enhancement ensures that the global symmetry of the mirror quiver matches that of the SQCD theory. In the SQCD theory, the chiral ring is generated by mesonic operators constructed from the fundamental fields \(Q_i\) and anti-fundamentals \(\tilde Q_j\) , which transform in the bifundamental representation of \[ SU(F_1+N)\times SU(F_2+N). \] Under the duality, these mesons are mapped to gauge-invariant monopole operators in the planar Abelian theory. For instance, the meson \(Q_{F_1+N}\tilde Q_{F_2+N}\) is mapped to the monopole operator with magnetic flux \(+1\) under the topmost gauge node of the Abelian quiver. More generally, the remaining mesons are mapped to monopole operators characterized by two strings of \(+1\) magnetic fluxes, originating at the topmost node and propagating along the two upper diagonals of the planar quiver. There are precisely \((F_1+N)(F_2+N)\) such monopole operators, reproducing the bifundamental representation of the enhanced \(SU(F_1+N)\times SU(F_2+N)\) global symmetry. Finally, we note that in this class of dualities, the planar Abelian theory does not exhibit any mesonic chiral ring generators. This implies that, for all values of \(F_1\) and \(F_2\) , the SQCD theory does not admit dressed gauge-invariant monopole operators that would parametrize a non-compact moduli space.

Lastly, a bifundamental chiral contributes with a CS level of $(-\tfrac{N}{2},-\tfrac{N}{2})$ . Therefore, the total CS level is:

section_title: \texorpdfstring{$U(N)$ CS-SQCD with $[F_1+2N, F_2]$ Chiral Multiplets}{U(N) with [F1+2N,F2]}
content: We now consider the second case in (which is analogous to the third one), the $U(N)_{(-\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}, -\frac{F_1+F_2}{2}-N)}$ SQCD with $[F_1+2N, F_2]$ chiral multiplets: As before, we decompose this theory into fundamental QFT blocks: and dualize each block by exploiting the basic moves: The resulting generalized quiver obtained by gluing the dualized QFT blocks is shown below: The generalized quiver in can finally be written in the explicit Lagrangian form by using the planar UV completion of the $\mathcal{S}$ -walls and the definition of the planar bifundamental and fundamental blocks. The result is depicted in Figure . The salient features of this duality closely parallel those of the duality shown in Figure . As a result, the mapping between global symmetries and chiral ring generators proceeds in direct analogy with the discussion presented at the end of Section . [Footnote: In contrast to the SQCD theory with \([F_1+N,F_2+N]\) flavors, in the present case there is no enhancement of the topological symmetry for special values of \(F_1\) and \(F_2\).] There is, however, an important qualitative difference. In this case, the planar dual can admit an additional chiral ring generator, which from the point of view of the planar theory is a mesonic operator. One can show that such an operator exists if and only if \(F_2 < N\) . Under the duality, this operator is mapped to a gauge-invariant dressed monopole operator in the SQCD, carrying positive magnetic charge. This correspondence can be explicitly checked, for instance by expanding the superconformal index, which confirms that the same operator appears in the SQCD precisely for the same range \(F_2 < N\) . It is possible to derive planar abelian duals for SQCD theories with $SU(N)$ gauge group or with $U(N)$ gauge group and CS levels $(k,k + \ell N)$ for any $\ell$ by applying Witten's $SL(2,\mathbb{Z})$ action on both sides of the dualities described above. \newpage

A natural question is whether we could exchange the two operations, i.e.: if we could take the planar limit of the SQCD theory. It is possible to directly take the planar limit of the SQCD theory, and follow the real mass and Higgsing at the level of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function.

section_title: An Example: The Planar Limit of Electric" CS-SQCD\texorpdfstring{$_3$}{3}
content: In Section , we started from the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pair of the $U(N)$ SQCD and considered the real mass deformation leading to the $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror duality relating the chiral $U(N)$ SQCD to its planar quiver dual pair, depicted in Figure . A natural question is whether we could exchange the two operations, i.e.: if we could take the planar limit of the SQCD theory. It is possible to directly take the planar limit of the SQCD theory, and follow the real mass and Higgsing at the level of the $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function. However, since these quivers are (generically) overbalanced, this is a tedious exercise for general values of $N$ and $F$ . The dualization algorithm allows us to reach the planar limit of such theories and is the subject of this section. To achieve this, we consider the chiral limit of the mirror of SQCD, namely: We decompose this theory into fundamental blocks, dualize each of them, and then glue back the result by implementing the Identity-walls. These steps are depicted in the following figure: Notice that the quiver considered in actually does not follow the rule prescribed at the beginning of the section since it contains two tails built with asymmetric $U(N)\times U(M)$ bifundamentals. However, we can bypass the problem of dualizing asymmetric bifundamental blocks by noticing that the two tails should be regarded as $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{S}^{-1}$ -walls. These walls do not need to be dualized, and in the last step, they fuse to Identity-walls with two other walls coming from the dualization of the flavor blocks. The resulting quiver can then be written explicitly in Lagrangian form, providing the planar mirror description for the original linear quiver gauge theory ( ), as shown in Figure . Interestingly, in the planar dual the flavor symmetry is not the naive $U(1)^{F+2N}/U(1)$ , but instead enhances to \[ S\!\left[U(F-1)\times U(2)^N\right], \] as shown in . This provides a particularly striking example of topological symmetry enhancement. The emergence of the $U(F-1)$ factor admits a natural explanation using the rule introduced in the previous section: the central portion of the quiver consists of a chain of $U(N)$ gauge nodes, each coupled to $[N,N]$ flavors, with bifundamental fields oriented uniformly along the quiver. By contrast, the appearance of the multiple $U(2)$ factors is a genuinely new feature of this duality. This enhancement involves topological symmetries associated with \emph{non-adjacent} gauge nodes and is therefore intrinsically non-local, unlike the local mechanism responsible for the $U(F-1)$ enhancement. Remarkably, both types of enhancement are simultaneously realized and made manifest in the planar dual description.

section_title: An Example: Circular Quivers
content: Our final example is that of an $\mathcal{N}=2$ circular quiver ( \textit{a la} ). Even in the absence of a fully refined $\mathbf{S}^3_b$ partition function, we can apply the dualization algorithm to obtain $\mathcal{N}=2$ chiral-planar mirror dual pairs. Concretely, we consider an $\mathcal{N}=4$ circular quiver of length $3$ and its corresponding mirror dual, shown in Figure . Upon considering a suitable real mass deformation, we flow to the $\mathcal{N}=2$ mirror dual pair, shown in Figure . Alternatively, one may arrive at the duality starting directly from the $\mathcal{N}=2$ circular quiver and applying the algorithm directly.

following_context: The results of this chapter strongly suggest that planar Abelian dual descriptions exist for a broad class of three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver gauge theories. While we do not provide a formal proof that all $\mathcal{N}=2$ quivers admit such a description, our findings indicate that this possibility is far more general than might have been expected \textit{a priori} . section_title: Going Beyond the Algorithm
content: The results of this chapter strongly suggest that planar Abelian dual descriptions exist for a broad class of three-dimensional $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver gauge theories. While we do not provide a formal proof that all $\mathcal{N}=2$ quivers admit such a description, our findings indicate that this possibility is far more general than might have been expected \textit{a priori} . In particular, the strategy illustrated in Section —based on real mass deformations of known $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror pairs—appears to be applicable whenever the parent $\mathcal{N}=4$ theories admit a Lagrangian description. In all cases analyzed, this procedure leads to a consistent $\mathcal{N}=2$ chiral-planar mirror duality. [Footnote: This argument relies on the availability of a Lagrangian description of the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror theories. For many interesting $\mathcal{N}=4$ SCFTs such a description is not known, and in those cases neither the partition function nor the equations of motion are under sufficient control to directly apply the present strategy.] From this perspective, the assumptions required to implement the algorithm in the examples studied in this work should be viewed as primarily technical rather than fundamental. Indeed, many of these restrictions can already be relaxed by performing a direct real mass analysis, as in Section , or may be overcome by developing a more refined version of the algorithm. All examples presented in this work are derived from known Lagrangian $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror dualities and rely on a controlled real mass deformation analysis. Nevertheless, many additional generalizations appear possible and remain to be explored. In closing, it is worth emphasizing that several of the remaining limitations of the present algorithm may be addressed by extending it with additional moves already known in the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror dualization framework. Implementing the appropriate supersymmetry-breaking deformations of these moves could lead to a significantly more powerful and systematic construction of planar abelian duals for general $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver gauge theories. \newpage

Indeed, many of these restrictions can already be relaxed by performing a direct real mass analysis, as in Section , or may be overcome by developing a more refined version of the algorithm. All examples presented in this work are derived from known Lagrangian $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror dualities and rely on a controlled real mass deformation analysis. Nevertheless, many additional generalizations appear possible and remain to be explored. In closing, it is worth emphasizing that several of the remaining limitations of the present algorithm may be addressed by extending it with additional moves already known in the $\mathcal{N}=4$ mirror dualization framework. Implementing the appropriate supersymmetry-breaking deformations of these moves could lead to a significantly more powerful and systematic construction of planar abelian duals for general $\mathcal{N}=2$ quiver gauge theories. \newpage
